prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
You probably believe that your future self will feel basically the same about the things you care about today—that the values and priorities you hold now are the “real” ones and that tomorrow you’ll simply be a wiser, better-funded version of the same person.  
In reality, the continuity you feel is largely an illusion manufactured by memory; psychological research shows core preferences and even personality traits drift far more than most people predict. The plans you’re making for that future “you” are therefore being written for a stranger who happens to share your name and Social Security number.
